diagrams.title: Spatial Domain to Frequency Domain Transformation
diagrams.description: A block diagram showing how an image in the spatial domain (pixel grid) is transformed into the frequency domain using Fourier Transform, and how inverse transform recovers the spatial domain
elements: Input Image f(x,y) - shown as a 2D grid of pixels with intensity values
Fourier Transform Block F(u,v) = F{f(x,y)} - mathematical transformation process
Frequency Spectrum Display - 2D array showing magnitude of frequency components
Center-low frequencies, edges-high frequencies visualized as concentric circles
Inverse Fourier Transform Block f(x,y) = F^{-1}{F(u,v)} - reconstruction process
Output Reconstructed Image - should match input
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the bidirectional flow between spatial and frequency domains and the role of Fourier Transform as an intermediary representation

diagrams.title: 2D Fourier Transform Process Flow
diagrams.description: Step-by-step visualization of how 2D DFT is computed from an image
elements: Input Image f(x,y) of size M×N
First Transform: Apply 1D DFT to each row → intermediate M×N matrix
Second Transform: Apply 1D DFT to each column of intermediate → final F(u,v)
Frequency Matrix F(u,v) with complex values
Magnitude Spectrum |F(u,v)| computed
Phase Spectrum ∠F(u,v) computed
DC Component at F(0,0) - top-left corner after shifting
diagrams.purpose: Students learn the sequential nature of 2D DFT computation and understand that it can be decomposed into two 1D transforms

diagrams.title: Frequency Spectrum Visualization
diagrams.description: Shows the structure of a 2D Fourier spectrum with labeled frequency regions
elements: Center origin (0,0) marked with DC component (highest magnitude)
Low frequencies region: Inner circle near center - smooth gradients, background
Mid frequencies: Middle ring - texture patterns
High frequencies: Outer region - edges, fine details, noise
Four quadrants labeled: Quadrant I (u>0,v>0), Quadrant II (u<0,v>0), Quadrant III (u<0,v<0), Quadrant IV (u>0,v<0)
Spectrum usually shifted so DC is at center for visualization
Brightness indicates magnitude - brighter = stronger frequency component
diagrams.purpose: Students learn to interpret frequency spectrum images and identify which frequencies correspond to which image features

diagrams.title: Frequency Domain Filter Types
diagrams.description: Comparison of three basic frequency domain filters with their spatial equivalents
elements: Ideal Low-Pass Filter: Circular cutoff passing only low frequencies, sharp cutoff
Ideal High-Pass Filter: Blocks low frequencies, passes only outer high frequencies
Ideal Band-Pass Filter: Ring-shaped, passes mid-range frequencies only
Butterworth variants: Smooth transition curves instead of sharp cutoff
Gaussian variants: Smooth bell-curve filtering
Each filter shown with: Frequency response H(u,v), 3D surface plot, resulting filtered spectrum
diagrams.purpose: Students understand different filter characteristics and their effects on image features (smoothing vs edge enhancement)

diagrams.title: FFT vs DFT Computation Complexity
diagrams.description: Comparison diagram showing computational advantage of FFT over DFT
elements: DFT: O(N²) operations shown as N×N grid of computations
FFT (Radix-2): O(N log N) operations shown as butterfly diagram with log₂N stages
For N=1024: DFT ≈ 1,048,576 operations, FFT ≈ 10,240 operations
100x speedup shown visually
Butterfly computation unit: 2-input, 2-output with twiddle factors
Stage structure: N/2 butterflies per stage, log₂N stages
diagrams.purpose: Students understand why FFT is essential for practical image processing and the dramatic efficiency improvement

charts.title: DFT vs FFT Comparison Table
charts.content: DFT: O(N²) complexity, Direct implementation, Slow for large images, Simple concept; FFT: O(N log N) complexity, Recursive divide-and-conquer, 100x+ faster, Requires power-of-2 sizes, Same mathematical result

charts.title: Frequency Domain Filtering Process
charts.content: Input Image → Pad Image (M×N → 2M×2N) → Compute FFT → Center Frequencies → Apply Filter H(u,v) → Multiply F(u,v) × H(u,v) → Compute IFFT → Crop to Original Size → Output Filtered Image

charts.title: Filter Types and Their Effects
charts.content: Low-Pass Filter: Passes Low frequencies (smooth areas), Blocks High frequencies (edges), Effect: Smoothing/Blurring/Noise Reduction; High-Pass Filter: Passes High frequencies, Blocks Low frequencies, Effect: Edge Enhancement/Sharpening; Band-Pass Filter: Passes Mid-range frequencies, Blocks both low and high, Effect: Texture extraction/Noise removal

charts.title: Properties of 2D Fourier Transform
charts.content: Linearity: F{af(x,y) + bg(x,y)} = aF(u,v) + bG(u,v); Shift: f(x-x₀,y-y₀) ↔ F(u,v)e^{-j2π(ux₀/M + vy₀/N)}; Convolution: f(x,y)*g(x,y) ↔ F(u,v)G(u,v); Rotation: f(r,θ+θ₀) ↔ F(ω,φ+θ₀); Separability: 2D transform = 1D row transform × 1D column transform

branches: Fundamentals: Spatial Domain (pixels x,y), Frequency Domain (u,v), Transformation F(u,v)
Applications: Filtering (smooth/sharpen), Feature Detection, Image Restoration, Compression
Properties: Linearity, Shift, Convolution, Rotation, Separability
Implementation: DFT (O(N²)), FFT (O(N log N)), 2D decomposition
mindMaps.purpose: Provides comprehensive overview connecting mathematical foundations to practical image processing applications
